info.parenttitle: B3.2 Structural systems application and selection (HL only)

markdown:
## Diagramming Structural Forces


<definition term="Force Diagram">A **force diagram** (or **free-body diagram**) shows all the forces acting on a structure or object. These include **applied loads**, **reactions at supports**, and **internal forces** like tension and compression.</definition>

![How force is represented in a diagram (accessdl)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/78f5014cf619b7233e2c538c51a976dae4634966-500x350.png)

<callout type="note">Copy this link and watch this video to understand how to **draw** and **interpret** **force diagrams**: https://youtu.be/29YPIvj1zjc?si=aM2lJ5vTk6lhNWFv</callout>

### Key Principles


1. **Forces are vectors**: They have both **magnitude** and **direction**.
2. **Forces in the same direction** add up.
3. **Forces in opposite directions** are subtracted.
4. **Resultant Force** is the overall effect of all forces acting on an object. 
  1. E.g. If one horse pulls a cart with 4 N and another with 3 N in the same direction → **Resultant = 7 N**
  2. If opposing (tug of war): 4 N left – 3 N right → **Resultant = 1 N left**

<callout type="note">#### Static Equilibrium


A structure is **in equilibrium** when:

* **Σ Vertical Forces = 0**
* **Σ Moments = 0**

If an object isn’t moving, the **resultant force = 0 N**.

![Balanced forces acting on a book — the weight is exactly countered by the normal reaction force (1 N up, 1 N down) (BBC)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/5ce564d054291441fc08ad9a9d7e28d8566991c6-624x217.png)</callout>

<callout type="tip">When creating a force diagram, always start by **identifying** **all the forces acting on the structure**, including external loads and internal reactions.</callout>

<callout type="example">A 100 N load sits in the middle of a 2 m beam supported at both ends.

**Step 1: Diagram**

* Draw a horizontal beam with supports at both ends.
* Show a 100 N downward force in the centre.

**Step 2: Calculate support reactions**

* Load is symmetric → Each support carries **50 N upward**
* Σ Forces = 50 N + 50 N – 100 N = 0 N</callout>

<callout type="tip">#### Forces vs Acceleration


* Use **Newton's 2nd Law** if movement is involved:
* $\text{Force (F)} = \text{Mass (m)} \times \text{Acceleration (a)}$
* If acceleration = 0, then the **resultant force = 0 N** (balanced).
* If unbalanced, the object will accelerate in the direction of the resultant force.</callout>

![Forces acting on a skier: weight is balanced by the combination of friction and the normal reaction force while descending a slope (BBC)](https://cdn.sanity.io/images/wla69xp2/production/50a7231402a1f97f72a3f3d9b142d1fdc569d345-624x237.png)

<callout type="warning">* Forgetting to **include support reactions**
* Misjudging direction (e.g. drawing all forces down)
* Confusing **moment** with **force**</callout>
